beats.prompt: Cumulative recall: match each expectation result to its value.

pairs.left: E[X]=Σx·P(x) is the fair price of a

feedback.correct: E[X]=Σx·P(x) — weight each outcome by its probability, sum up; that's the fair price of any bet. (EV L1.)
hints: One card asks what E[X] is the fair price of; one asks what you weight outcomes by.
Σx·P(x) multiplies each value by its probability — that's the weight; E[X] is the break-even price.
bet; probability; 7/2 (#EV1).

beats.prompt: The stock is 100. In one step it goes to 120 or 80. A call struck at 100 pays 20 or 0. Which probability do you weight the payoffs by to price it?

options: The real-world probability p that the stock rises
A weight q computed from u, d, R (not p), then discounted
50/50 — by symmetry the two outcomes are equally likely

The real-world probability p that the st.note: Let's test it — but you were never told p. The astonishing result: the price doesn't depend on it. No-arbitrage forces a different weight q=(R−d)/(u−d) that replaces the real odds entirely (#13).

A weight q computed from u, d, R (not p).note: Good instinct — price comes from replication / no-arbitrage. Here q=(R−d)/(u−d)=1/2, then discount by 1/R. The real p never appears (#13).

50/50 — by symmetry the two outcomes are.note: Let's test it — you land on 1/2, but for the wrong reason. It isn't symmetry of the real odds; it's q=(R−d)/(u−d), which is 1/2 only for this tree. Change u,d,R and q moves — the q-drill gives 3/5 (#15).

beats.prompt: Tap the up-node (S=120) or down-node (S=80) in the one-step lattice. The side panel reads S, the q-weight, the rolled-back option value, and the replicating portfolio {Δ, B}. Tap "Reveal hedge cost" to see hedge cost = Δ·S + B = price at the root.

hero.structuralReadout: One-step binomial tree: S₀=100 branches to 120 (up) or 80 (down); call pays 20 or 0; hedge cost = price = 10.

feedback.correct: The cost of the hedge is the price — regardless of which branch. The replicating portfolio {Δ=1/2, B=−40} costs ½·100 − 40 = 10 = call price (#13).
hints: Tap the up-node (S=120) or down-node (S=80) to read S, the q-weight, and the rolled-back option value.
The side panel shows the replicating portfolio {Δ, B}; tap 'Reveal hedge cost' to see Δ·S + B at the root.
Hedge cost = ½·100 − 40 = 10 = price. The real odds never entered (#13).

beats.prompt: Three lenses on option pricing — read, then continue.

interaction.title: Three lenses: q, replication, and the discount
interaction.body: ① Risk-neutral weight: q=(R−d)/(u−d). This is NOT the real up-probability p; it is a no-arbitrage weight forced by the tree. Example: u=3/2, d=1/2, R=11/10 gives q=(11/10−1/2)/(3/2−1/2)=3/5 (#15 — worked example only). ② Replication: build a portfolio of Δ shares and a bond B that copies the option payoff at every node — Δ=(V_u−V_d)/(S(u−d)), B=price−Δ·S. ③ Discount: price=(1/R)·E_q[payoff]. Replication and the q-measure are two views of the same answer; the real p never appears in either.

feedback.correct: Three lenses, one answer: q=(R−d)/(u−d) is the no-arbitrage weight; Δ and B replicate the payoff; price=(1/R)·E_q. The real odds never enter.
hints: Lens ①: q=(R−d)/(u−d) is forced by no-arbitrage, not the real up-prob.
Lens ②: replicate with Δ shares and bond B so the portfolio matches the option at every node.
Lens ③: price=(1/R)·E_q[payoff]. Continue when ready.

beats.prompt: Canonical tree: S₀=100, u=6/5, d=4/5, R=1. Compute the risk-neutral weight q=(R−d)/(u−d).

fields.label: Risk-neutral q for the canonical tree

feedback.correct: q=(R−d)/(u−d): (1−4/5)/(6/5−4/5) = (1/5)/(2/5) = 1/2. The no-arb weight, not the real up-prob (#13).
hints: Numerator: R−d=1−4/5=1/5; denominator: u−d=6/5−4/5=2/5.

beats.prompt: A stock at 100 moves to 120 or 80 in one step; R=1. A call struck at 100 pays 20 or 0. Which method gives its arbitrage-free price?

Risk-neutral pricing.note: Discount the q-weighted payoff: q=(R−d)/(u−d)=(1−4/5)/(2/5)=1/2, then price=(1/1)·(1/2·20+1/2·0)=10. q comes from no-arbitrage, not the real odds — that's why you never needed p (#13).

Linearity / indicators.note: Linearity of expectation under the real p doesn't price an option — that's the marquee trap. You must use the no-arbitrage weight q and discount by 1/R. Averaging 20 and 0 under real odds is not the arbitrage-free price (#13).

Conditioning.note: Conditioning on the first step is the right shape, but only the q-weighted, discounted version is arbitrage-free. A plain conditional average under p still smuggles in the real odds (#13).

Prior update.note: There is no prior and no evidence here. q is a change of measure forced by no-arbitrage, not Bayes' rule — you solve (R−d)/(u−d), not Bayes' theorem. (The continuity report flags this exact false-analogy; #13.)

beats.prompt: Same method, different costume. Match each result to its tree.

pairs.left: What is the same in both?

feedback.correct: Discounted E_q — the same formula regardless of r. q=(R−d)/(u−d) absorbs the interest rate; the real odds never enter. Method invariant across costumes (#13, #14).
hints: Plug each tree's u, d, R into q=(R−d)/(u−d) — what do you get for both?
Both trees use discounted E_q — the real odds never enter (#13, #14).

beats.prompt: Transfer challenge: same tree as #13 (S=100, u=6/5, d=4/5, R=1), but price the PUT struck at K=100. Then check put-call parity.

interaction.scenario: Put on the canonical tree: P_u=max(100−120,0)=0 up, P_d=max(100−80,0)=20 down. Use q=1/2. Find price=(1/R)·E_q, delta Δ=(P_u−P_d)/(S(u−d)), bond B=price−Δ·S, and parity gap C−P−(S−K).

hints: For a put: P_u=max(K−S_u,0)=0, P_d=max(K−S_d,0)=20; use q=1/2 from the call tree.

beats.prompt: Required mastery: canonical call (#13). Give q, the call price, delta Δ, and bond B. Then confirm hedge cost = price.

interaction.scenario: Canonical call: S₀=100, u=6/5, d=4/5, R=1, K=100. C_u=max(120−100,0)=20, C_d=max(80−100,0)=0. Find q=(R−d)/(u−d), price=(1/R)·E_q, Δ=(C_u−C_d)/(S(u−d)), B=price−Δ·S.

feedback.correct: Replication and the q-measure agree: q=1/2 by no-arbitrage, price=(1/1)·(1/2·20+1/2·0)=10, hedge cost=½·100−40=10=price. The real odds never entered (#13).

beats.interviewNote: Interview framing: 'Price a one-step binomial call' is a Green-Book classic — the key is q=(R−d)/(u−d), the risk-neutral weight (NOT the real up-probability); then price=(1/R)·E_q[payoff]. Replication gives the same answer: Δ shares + bond B such that Δ·S_u+B=C_u and Δ·S_d+B=C_d; the cost Δ·S+B equals the price. Canonical tree (u=6/5,d=4/5,R=1,K=100): q=1/2, price=10, Δ=1/2, B=−40. The real up-probability never enters. A put on the same tree gives q=1/2, price=10, Δ=−1/2, B=+60, and parity C−P=0=S−K.
beats.prompt: One method, two lenses — pricing by replication and the risk-neutral measure.

feedback.correct: q=(R−d)/(u−d) is a no-arbitrage weight, not the real up-probability and not a Bayes posterior. price = hedge cost = (1/R)·E_q. Next: multi-step trees (L5).
hints: Read each recap card.
q is a no-arbitrage weight; replication and E_q are the same answer viewed differently.
Tap to finish.
